title: $XDG_CONFIG_HOME support for registries.conf
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

Also, there is a running list of known issues in the [Podman Troubleshooting Guide](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/troubleshooting.md),
please reference that page before opening a new issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind feature

**Description**

<!--
Briefly describe the problem you are having in a few paragraphs.
-->

Would you consider adding $XDG_CONFIG_HOME support for the registries.conf file in order for podman to locate registries.conf files located at a specified XDG_CONFIG_HOME? Files like storage.conf seem to also support this behavior while registries.conf does not for some reason.

comments.body:
Thanks for reaching out, @p0da.  I recall conversations about that a longer while ago but my memory is fading.

@mtrmac has a much better memory :)

I can’t see any earlier _design_ conversation about this, just a few offhand comments stating that that’s what it is.

The default location is already consistent with $XDG_CONFIG_HOME use, so switching should be seamless for default installs that don’t modify the variable (but it would, of course, break those use a different value of $XDG_CONFIG_HOME currently).

I really have no preference about this at all. Purely personally — if you care to do the work of changing the code and updating documentation throughout, why not.
Makes sense. I would say that from a consistency POV this is fairly important as it is feels very unintuitive to have some config files work with a different $XDG_CONFIG_HOME while others don't.
Moved the issue to containers/image where the work would have to happen.

@vrothberg mind if I take a look at this?
Thanks

It turns out, as described in https://github.com/containers/image/pull/2931 , that we can’t add this feature without breaking our stable API commitment.

So, it might happen _eventually_, but it’s very unlikely to happen soon.

@mtrmac Regardless, thanks for looking into it! 

I really hope it can be solved sometime, because it seems uintegrated and odd that this is hardcoded - especially given you embrace all other aspects of systemd/FreeDesktop.

.. but hey! Systemd itself doesn't even respect it sometimes: https://github.com/systemd/systemd/issues/24960 even if thats *pretty* wack!

EDIT: and honestly since registries.conf is what is most likely going to be *most commonly* overridden by a user with aliases or new registries I find this bug pretty foundational to user customizability. Its hardcoded incompatability with the XDG base spec. It was a design mistake not to return an error on the config which had many reasons incl but not limited to permissions.
This is begin worked on for Podman 6 https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/contrib/design-docs/config-file-parsing.md
@Luap99 I still worry about that API break. Let’s see…

What is the concern?

A user has ~/.config/cotnainers/registries.conf and a custom XDG_CONFIG_HOME that points elsewhere? So then we break him as we only read $XDG_CONFIG_HOME/containers/registries.conf? 
What the alternative? Anything else means we can never fix this.
If a user is having a custom XDG_CONFIG_HOME then they are likely happy to see to have one less thing under .config instead IMO.

If it is only about breaking the go API then I am not sure what I would have to break, my code is still WIP but so far I have not seen anything that would need a public API change in pkg/sysregistriesv2.

> The whole context of this is users that set XDG_CONFIG_HOME to a non-default value. If the user does that, and the value is invalid (e.g. a typo), I think the user must be told, and the operation must fail.

I am not sure I agree on that saying, there is no requirement that XDG_CONFIG_HOME must exists AFAIK as such raising an error on non existence seems not correct. And thinking this further you can say the exact same thing about HOME which the code currently happily ignores the lookup error on so XDG_CONFIG_HOME is not special even if we would do that.

My stubborn commitment is to the Go API.

The argument that there’s no discernible difference between a typo in `$XDG_CONFIG_HOME` and correct `$XDG_CONFIG_HOME` pointing to a location with no config file is quite strong.

… looking further, the error path is about `storage/pkg/homedir.GetConfigHome` noticing that the variable points at _someone else’s_ home directory (as happens with some uses of `su`).

Now `pkg/configfile` does not call that… so right now that does not make a difference. Whether we _should_ have that sanity check again is a bit different question — we used to do that in `common/pkg/config`.

> Now pkg/configfile does not call that… so right now that does not make a difference. Whether we should have that sanity check again is a bit different question — we used to do that in common/pkg/config.

Which has been an explicit design decision for me due problems like https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/23818
For just reading config files we should not need to verify ownership or existence (if they are wrong it is the user problem not on us)
Now for writing files I do agree having some more protections is useful but pkg/configfile should not do that.

There is also something like https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/27921, whenever the expextation is valid there is a different question.

Anyhow off topic to registries.conf here, just noting down some of the reasons that cause my decisions.
